commit.message:
Fix route ordering by path specificity for ACME challenges (#1412)

Routes within a VirtualHost are now sorted by path length (descending)
so that more specific paths are matched first. This fixes ACME HTTP-01
challenges being misrouted when a catch-all "/" route was processed
before the "/.well-known/acme-challenge/..." route.

Envoy uses first-match-wins semantics, so without this sorting, the
order in which Ingress rules are processed determines route priority,
which can cause unexpected behavior.

commit.message:
Fix handling of endpoints with no ready addresses (#1397)

When Kourier reconciles an Ingress with endpoints that have no ready
addresses, it creates Envoy clusters with empty endpoints [{}], which
Envoy rejects, triggering a persistent error loop.

The issue occurs because lbEndpointsForKubeEndpoints() returns nil for
endpoints with only NotReadyAddresses, but envoy.NewCluster() creates
invalid clusters with this nil value.

Fix: Skip snapshot updates when endpoints exist but aren't ready. The
tracker already watches Endpoints and will trigger reconciliation when
they become ready.

This prevents invalid Envoy configs and "unknown weighted cluster" errors.

issues.title: Recommended approach for exposing Knative services to separate external networks
issues.body:
Hey! I'm trying to set up Kourier so that some Knative services are only reachable from a private network while others are also reachable from a public/internet-facing network.

The two approaches I've been looking at:

**Option A — Dual LoadBalancers, same Envoy pod:**
Create a second LoadBalancer Service targeting the internal listener (port 8081) for private traffic, while the existing one handles public traffic on 8080/8443. Use `cluster-local` labels to control which services appear on the external listeners.

**Option B — Two separate Kourier deployments with different ingress classes:**
Run two independent Kourier controller + gateway pairs, each with its own ingress class. Private services use one class, public services use the other.

Option B gives full isolation but seems to conflict with Knative's model where each Service gets a single ingress class annotation — you can't route the same service through both gateways without workarounds.

Option A reuses the existing internal/external listener split which seems designed for this, but I couldn't find documentation confirming it's a supported pattern.

A few questions:

1. Is there a recommended way to handle this? Are there other approaches I'm missing?

2. With option A — does the internal listener (8081) respect `http-protocol: Redirected` from config-network? A 301→HTTPS redirect on the internal side would be a problem if there's no internal TLS listener to handle it.

3. Is there interest in making multi-network setups more of a first-class feature? For example, per-listener TLS or per-listener redirect behavior.

Would appreciate any guidance. Thanks!

issues.title: Kourier controller fails to start with large numbers of services (7500+)
Routes not sorted by path specificity causing ACME HTTP-01 challenges to fail
issues.body:
When multiple Knative Ingress rules share the same hostname, Kourier appends routes to the VirtualHost in processing order without sorting by path specificity. Since Envoy uses [first-match-wins semantics](https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/configuration/http/http_conn_man/route_matching), a catch-all `/` route can match before more specific paths like `/.well-known/acme-challenge/...`, causing requests to be misrouted.

## Impact

ACME HTTP-01 certificate challenges fail because challenge requests are routed to the Knative service instead of the cert-manager solver pod. This breaks automatic TLS certificate provisioning.

## Root Cause

In `pkg/generator/ingress_translator.go`, the `addOrAppendVirtualHost()` function appends routes without considering path length:

```go
existing.Routes = append(existing.Routes, routes...)  // No sorting
```

When traffic rules (with `/` prefix) are processed before ACME rules (with `/.well-known/acme-challenge/...` prefix), the wrong route matches first.

## Related

- CI failure: https://prow.knative.dev/view/gs/knative-prow/logs/kourier-stable_serving_main_periodic/1996733114616909824
- Serving PR that exposed this: https://github.com/knative/serving/pull/16259

issues.title: Adjust liveness and readiness probes defaults
issues.body:
Per @maschmid observation in clusters with 100+ KSVC Kourier deployment can be prematurely marked as unhealthy due to aggressive `1s` timeout default on the current liveness probe. 



Taking a look at Envoy's deployment defaults in helm chart, the probing can be set to a lot milder values. In addition to raising `timeout`. 
https://github.com/envoyproxy/gateway/blob/main/charts/gateway-helm/templates/envoy-gateway-deployment.yaml#L64-L69

We could introduce a config nob to influence the current hard default. In addition, there could be per KSVC annotation if we found it useful.

issues.title: Add Support for EndpointSlices
issues.body:
Net-kourier currently uses the Endpoints API exclusively for service discovery. The Endpoints API was officially deprecated in Kubernetes 1.33 and users are encouraged to migrate to EndpointSlices, which is the more scalable and modern API for endpoint discovery.

## Current Implementation

The codebase currently:

1. Watches Endpoints via `endpointsinformer`
2. Retrieves Endpoints via `endpointsGetter`
3. Converts Endpoints to Envoy LbEndpoints
4. Has RBAC for `endpoints` but not `endpointslices`

## Benefits

- Avoids deprecation warnings in Kubernetes 1.33+
- Better scalability at large scale (EndpointSlices paginate, Endpoints don't)

## References

- Related: knative/serving#7701
- <https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/endpoint-slices/>
- <https://kubernetes.io/blog/2025/04/24/endpoints-deprecation/>

/kind enhancement
/assign

issues.title: Kourier fails to handle Ingress with duplicate domains across multiple rules

labels.name: kind/bug
labels.description: Categorizes issue or PR as related to a bug.

issues.body:
## Description

Kourier currently fails to correctly translate Knative Ingress resources that specify the same host/domain in multiple rules with different paths. This violates Envoy's requirement that domains must be unique across all VirtualHosts.

## Problem

When a Knative Ingress contains multiple rules with the same host but different paths, Kourier creates separate VirtualHosts for each rule, resulting in duplicate domain names. According to the [Envoy specification](https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/api-v3/config/route/v3/route_components.proto):

> A domain must be unique across all virtual hosts or the config will fail to load.

## Example Use Case

A realistic scenario is combining application traffic with ACME challenge handling:

```yaml
apiVersion: networking.internal.knative.dev/v1alpha1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: example
  namespace: default
spec:
  rules:
  - hosts: ["example.com"]
    http:
      paths:
      - path: "/"
        splits:
        - serviceName: app-svc
          servicePort: 80
  - hosts: ["example.com"]  # Same domain, different path
    http:
      paths:
      - path: "/.well-known/acme-challenge/token"
        splits:
        - serviceName: solver-svc
          servicePort: 8089
```

## Expected Behavior

Kourier must generate Envoy configuration that ensures each domain appears in exactly one VirtualHost, regardless of how the Ingress rules are structured.

## Current Behavior

Kourier creates separate VirtualHosts for each rule, causing duplicate domains which violates Envoy's requirements.

## Note

The Knative Ingress CRD allows duplicate hosts across rules (no validation prevents this). Kubernetes Ingress resources also permit this pattern, with controllers typically merging paths for the same host.

## Related

- https://github.com/knative/serving/issues/16258 - Similar issue where Route controller generates duplicate ACME challenge paths across ingress rules

/kind bug
/assign

issues.title: Kourier creates invalid Envoy config when endpoints have no ready addresses

labels.name: kind/bug
labels.description: Categorizes issue or PR as related to a bug.

issues.body:
## Problem

When Kourier reconciles an Ingress with endpoints that have no ready addresses, it creates Envoy clusters with empty endpoints `{}`, which Envoy rejects with "unknown weighted cluster" errors.

## Root Cause

`lbEndpointsForKubeEndpoints()` returns `nil` for endpoints with only NotReadyAddresses, but `envoy.NewCluster()` creates invalid clusters with this nil value instead of skipping cluster creation.

## Reproduction

This can occur when pods aren't ready during Ingress reconciliation:
- Application pods during initial deployment or scaling
- Any service with slow-starting containers

## Proposed Solution

Skip snapshot updates when endpoints exist but have no ready addresses. The tracker already watches Endpoints and will trigger reconciliation when they become ready.

issues.title: Add W3C Trace Context support for distributed tracing
issues.body:
Kourier currently only supports B3 headers for distributed tracing propagation. 
This limits interoperability with knative itself which does not support extracting context from the old zipkin headers anymore but only from the W3C Trace Context standard (traceparent/tracestate headers).

This behavior is already in place since knative 1.19, see:
- https://github.com/knative/pkg/blob/7e6a0208dd367d18db5fe2956b3ad151f252f941/observability/tracing/provider.go#L47-L52
- https://github.com/knative/serving/pull/15961

## Proposal:

Add a new configuration option to the `config-kourier` ConfigMap with two values:
- `b3` - B3 headers only (backward compatible)
- `b3-w3c` - Dual propagation: extracts from B3 first (fallback to W3C), injects both header formats (default)

This is possible thanks to the new `TraceContextOption` field added in envoy 1.36: 
- https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/v1.36.2/api-v3/config/trace/v3/zipkin.proto.html#envoy-v3-api-field-config-trace-v3-zipkinconfig-trace-context-option
- https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/version_history/v1.36/v1.36.0.html

That would mean we:
- enable interoperability with knative itself and other W3C-compliant observability tools
- maintain backward compatibility
- default to dual propagation for better compatibility

/kind enhancement
/assign

issues.title: net-kourier-controller goes into CrashLoopBackOff due to AKS API server throttling

labels.name: triage/accepted
labels.description: Issues which should be fixed (post-triage)

issues.body:
### tl;dr
Every once in a while, the `net-kourier-controller` pod goes into a CrashLoopBackOff because it tries to retrieve all endpoints at once, gets throttled by the AKS API server and is unable to recover until almost all `ksvc` and `king` resources are deleted.

### Versions
- AKS: `1.31.6` (Standard pricing tier)
- Knative Serving: `1.17.0`
- Kourier: `sha256:ab7885b64daf628a6b1e8f0aef572ec6752cd0497172963ffeec80cd870f5fcc`

### Description
We have a Knative Serving setup on our test AKS cluster that we use to create frontend previews generated by pull request validation builds - these get cleaned up regularly to keep the number of `ksvc` resources in check, though a couple hundred are usually present at a time, mostly around 300 or so.

At seemingly random intervals, however, the `net-kourier-controller` pod crashes, rendering the services inaccessible. When this happens, the pod log usually looks something like this:
```
2025/05/09 08:56:04 Registering 2 clients
2025/05/09 08:56:04 Registering 4 informer factories
2025/05/09 08:56:04 Registering 5 informers
2025/05/09 08:56:04 Registering 1 controllers
{"severity":"INFO","timestamp":"2025-05-09T08:56:04.891210919Z","logger":"net-kourier-controller","caller":"profiling/server.go:65","message":"Profiling enabled: false","commit":"bfecb96-dirty"}
{"severity":"INFO","timestamp":"2025-05-09T08:56:06.868086592Z","logger":"net-kourier-controller","caller":"leaderelection/context.go:47","message":"Running with Standard leader election","commit":"bfecb96-dirty"}
{"severity":"INFO","timestamp":"2025-05-09T08:56:10.131139801Z","logger":"net-kourier-controller","caller":"ingress/controller.go:222","message":"Priming the config with 269 ingresses","commit":"bfecb96-dirty"}
{"severity":"EMERGENCY","timestamp":"2025-05-09T08:57:05.227430746Z","logger":"net-kourier-controller","caller":"ingress/controller.go:252","message":"Failed prewarm ingress","commit":"bfecb96-dirty","error":"failed to translate ingress: failed to fetch service 'dg-customerservice/dg-customerservice-webapi-pr225822-00001': client rate limiter Wait returned an error: context canceled","stacktrace":"knative.dev/net-kourier/pkg/reconciler/ingress.NewController\n\tknative.dev/net-kourier/pkg/reconciler/ingress/controller.go:252\nknative.dev/pkg/injection/sharedmain.ControllersAndWebhooksFromCtors\n\tknative.dev/pkg@v0.0.0-20250117084104-c43477f0052b/injection/sharedmain/main.go:467\nknative.dev/pkg/injection/sharedmain.MainWithConfig\n\tknative.dev/pkg@v0.0.0-20250117084104-c43477f0052b/injection/sharedmain/main.go:272\nknative.dev/pkg/injection/sharedmain.MainWithContext\n\tknative.dev/pkg@v0.0.0-20250117084104-c43477f0052b/injection/sharedmain/main.go:209\nmain.main\n\tknative.dev/net-kourier/cmd/kourier/main.go:32\nruntime.main\n\truntime/proc.go:272"}
```
Then the pod would crash and restart - note that the service sometimes changes and is usually not corrupted in any way, it seems like this just happens to be the unlucky one that got throttled by the AKS API server. The new pod would then run into the same error and crash, and so on. The only way we've found so far to get it to run again is to delete all `ksvc` and `king` resources. That way, the pod will start properly and run - until it crashes again at some point.

### What we've tried so far
- Have both `net-kourier-controller` as well as the other Knative serving pods run with multiple replicas.
- Increase the resources the pod may use (we're currently at 1500 mCPUs and 1500 MiB of memory - though Kubernetes never killed the pod for a lack of resources).
- Set `KUBE_API_BURST` and `KUBE_API_QPS` environment variables in the `controller` container within the pod, but even setting them both to 1 had no effect.

None of these measures helped though, they neither prevented the crashing in the first place, nor did they allow the pod to recover when it did. We're currently evaluating switching to Contour to see if that might help, but we'd prefer to keep Kourier if possible. If there's any config options we've missed, or guidance where we might look into, we would hugely appreciate it, because our frontend teams depend heavily on Knative.

issues.title: Kourier at large scale

labels.name: lifecycle/frozen
labels.description: Indicates that an issue or PR should not be auto-closed due to staleness.

issues.body: **What's the issue?** We have started testing Kourier at large scale to see if deployment times are better than Istio(time for a KSVC to become ready to serve traffic). Deploy times are good with Kourier and it consistently takes less than 10 seconds for a newly added KSVC to become ready all the way up to 2000 KSVC. However, if you delete a KSVC and then you try to add a new KSVC, times are much slower and even with only 500 KSVC on the cluster it takes several minutes before the new KSVC is ready. Looking at the logs in the net-kourier-controller, you can see that it starts reconciling all of the Ingress on the cluster when you delete a KSVC and presumably this needs to finish before the new ingress can be created for our new KSVC. **Why is this a problem?** This leads to inconsistent deploy times for our workloads which creates an inconsistent user experience as sometimes its really quick and other times it could takes minutes to become ready. **Results** Here are the times it took for a single KSVC to become ready right after I deleted a different single KVSC alongside the number of KSVC that were on the cluster. **Why are we doing this?** We are running a cluster with Knative and Istio with 1500 KSVC and have started to run a problem with the time it's taking before new KSVC we add become ready (the ingress). We opened an issue for this here: https://github.com/knative/serving/issues/13247

issues.title: Envoy circuit breakers hit with HTTP/2

labels.name: lifecycle/frozen
labels.description: Indicates that an issue or PR should not be auto-closed due to staleness.

issues.body:
I use a custom client to send requests to Knative services with a Kourier gateway. It has a slow-start algorithm that gradually scales up concurrent requests until a configured limit is reached, or until error rates are too high. Under normal conditions, with HTTP/1.1, this can easily scale to 10k concurrent requests with just a few Kourier pods. Using HTTP/1.1 is more fragile as it could hit socket limits. However, if I switch that same config to HTTP/2, once the concurrency hits approximately 1000 (maybe slightly lower), Kourier / envoy responds with:

```
HTTP/2.0 503 Service Unavailable
Content-Length: 81
Content-Type: text/plain
Date: Tue, 10 May 2022 01:58:16 GMT
Server: envoy
X-Envoy-Overloaded: true

upstream connect error or disconnect/reset before headers. reset reason: overflow
```

If I reduce the concurrency, but increase the number of clients sending requests, once the total concurrent requests goes above 1000, the same result happens. This might depend on the number of upstream service pods, I was testing with just 2.

Is there a way to tune this setting in the Kourier config? Ideally, if requests per socket connection need to be limited, it would allow spawning more connections when the limit is reached.

edit: to clarify, the upstream Service is not changing here, just how the client connects. The upstream service port is defined as a named port (`h2c`).

pullRequests.title: Fix route ordering by path specificity
pullRequests.body:
Routes within a VirtualHost are now sorted by path length (descending) so that more specific paths are matched first. This fixes ACME HTTP-01 challenges being misrouted when a catch-all "/" route was processed before the "/.well-known/acme-challenge/..." route.

[Envoy uses first-match-wins semantics](https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/configuration/http/http_conn_man/route_matching), so without this sorting, the order in which Ingress rules are processed determines route priority, which can cause unexpected behavior.


## Changes

- :bug: Sort routes within each VirtualHost by path length (descending) so more specific paths are matched first

/kind bug

Fixes #1411
Relates to https://github.com/knative/serving/pull/16259

## Release Note

```release-note
Fixed path matching priority when an Ingress has multiple rules for the same domain.
```

/cc @dsimansk

pullRequests.title: Prevent duplicate domains across Ingress rules
pullRequests.body:
/kind bug

Fixes #1398

## Description

Kourier previously failed to correctly translate Knative Ingress resources that specify the same host/domain in multiple rules with different paths. This resulted in duplicate domain names across multiple VirtualHosts, violating Envoy's requirement that domains must be unique.

When this bug occurs, Envoy logs show repeated errors like:
```
[warning][config] [source/extensions/config_subscription/grpc/grpc_subscription_impl.cc:138]
gRPC config for type.googleapis.com/envoy.config.route.v3.RouteConfiguration rejected:
Only unique values for domains are permitted. Duplicate entry of domain example.com in route external_services
```

## Changes

- Changed VirtualHost storage from slices to maps keyed by hostname, enabling automatic deduplication
- Routes now grouped by domain rather than by Ingress rule
- Added tests for duplicate domains, multiple domains/paths, and mixed visibility
- Updated existing test expectations for Domain-based naming

## Example Use Case

This fix enables realistic scenarios like combining application traffic with ACME challenge handling:

```yaml
apiVersion: networking.internal.knative.dev/v1alpha1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: example
  namespace: default
spec:
  rules:
    - hosts: ["example.com"]
      http:
        paths:
          - path: "/"
            splits:
              - serviceName: app-svc
                servicePort: 80
    - hosts: ["example.com"] # Same domain, different path
      http:
        paths:
          - path: "/.well-known/acme-challenge/token"
            splits:
              - serviceName: solver-svc
                servicePort: 8089
```

Previously, this would create two VirtualHosts both claiming `example.com`, causing Envoy to reject the configuration. Now, routes are merged into a single VirtualHost.

## Related

- Related upstream issue: <https://github.com/knative/serving/issues/16258>

## Release Note

```release-note
Fixed duplicate domain handling in Kourier when multiple Ingress rules specify the same host with different paths (e.g., app traffic + ACME challenges). Routes are now correctly merged into a single VirtualHost per domain, satisfying Envoy's uniqueness requirement.
```

pullRequests.title: Fix handling of endpoints with no ready addresses
pullRequests.body:
When Kourier reconciles an Ingress with endpoints that have no ready addresses, it creates Envoy clusters with empty endpoints [{}], which Envoy rejects, triggering an error loop.

The issue occurs because lbEndpointsForKubeEndpoints() returns nil for endpoints with only NotReadyAddresses, but envoy.NewCluster() creates invalid clusters with this nil value.

Fix: Skip snapshot updates when endpoints exist but aren't ready. The tracker already watches Endpoints and will trigger reconciliation when they become ready.

This prevents invalid Envoy configs and "unknown weighted cluster" errors.

# Changes


- :bug: Fix handling of endpoints with no ready addresses

/kind bug
Fixes #1396

**Supplement**

Disabled [the bootstrap access_log](https://github.com/knative-extensions/net-kourier/blob/cf879dd3cadb8c0a92cf81ef1782e9808cfcc62d/config/200-bootstrap.yaml#L120) and verified the following:

- 1:  By default, logs are output in Envoy's default format.
    ```
    $ kubectl -n kourier-system logs 3scale-kourier-gateway-77cf57795b-9tg2j --tail=3
    [2025-05-31T08:58:36.928Z] "GET /ready HTTP/1.1" 200 - 0 5 0 0 "-" "kube-probe/1.32" "dbaf942f-75e6-4537-8ca7-d31a76eff74a" "internalkourier" "127.0.0.1:9901"
    [2025-05-31T08:58:39.044Z] "GET /ready HTTP/1.1" 200 - 0 5 0 0 "-" "kube-probe/1.32" "625e2066-6c5f-426c-b011-fd1d79b84d31" "internalkourier" "127.0.0.1:9901"
    [2025-05-31T08:58:41.928Z] "GET /ready HTTP/1.1" 200 - 0 5 0 0 "-" "kube-probe/1.32" "af6d0322-b6f1-4773-ae75-c3c7d5a629e3" "internalkourier" "127.0.0.1:9901"
    ```
- 2: The `service-access-log-template` can be enabled.
    ```
    $ cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
    apiVersion: v1
    kind: ConfigMap
    metadata:
    name: config-kourier
    namespace: knative-serving
    data:
    service-access-log-template: >
        {
        "httpRequest": {
        "requestMethod": "%REQ(:METHOD)%",
        "requestUrl": "%REQ(:PATH)%",
        "protocol": "%PROTOCOL%",
        "requestSize": "%BYTES_RECEIVED%",
        "responseSize": "%BYTES_SENT%",
        "status": %RESPONSE_CODE%,
        "latency": "%DURATION%",
        "userAgent": "%REQ(USER-AGENT)%",
        "remoteIp": "%REQ(X-FORWARDED-FOR)%",
        "serverIp": "%UPSTREAM_HOST%",
        "referer": "%REQ(REFERER)%" },
        "traceId": "%REQ(X-REQUEST-ID)%",
        "authority": "%REQ(:AUTHORITY)%"
        }
    EOF
    configmap/config-kourier configured
    $ kubectl rollout restart -n knative-serving deployment net-kourier-controller
    deployment.apps/net-kourier-controller restarted
    $
    $ kubectl -n kourier-system logs 3scale-kourier-gateway-77cf57795b-9tg2j --tail=3
    { "httpRequest": { "requestMethod": "GET", "requestUrl": "/ready", "protocol": "HTTP/1.1", "requestSize": "0", "responseSize": "5", "status": 200, "latency": "0", "userAgent": "kube-probe/1.32", "remoteIp": "-", "serverIp": "127.0.0.1:9901", "referer": "-" }, "traceId": "a5a49ac0-41ee-45b4-9eff-fb71f4e45d5a", "authority": "internalkourier" }
    { "httpRequest": { "requestMethod": "GET", "requestUrl": "/ready", "protocol": "HTTP/1.1", "requestSize": "0", "responseSize": "5", "status": 200, "latency": "0", "userAgent": "kube-probe/1.32", "remoteIp": "-", "serverIp": "127.0.0.1:9901", "referer": "-" }, "traceId": "a1344bda-319b-4bc5-8370-d84dde2141b6", "authority": "internalkourier" }
    { "httpRequest": { "requestMethod": "GET", "requestUrl": "/ready", "protocol": "HTTP/1.1", "requestSize": "0", "responseSize": "5", "status": 200, "latency": "0", "userAgent": "kube-probe/1.32", "remoteIp": "-", "serverIp": "127.0.0.1:9901", "referer": "-" }, "traceId": "3efeafc8-bf85-41a9-97ce-e027c62b37a6", "authority": "internalkourier" }
    ```
- 3: The `service-access-log-template` can be disabled.
    ```
    $ kubectl patch configmap config-kourier -n knative-serving --type merge -p '{"data": {"service-access-log-template": null}}'

    $

    deployment.apps/net-kourier-controller restarted
    $

    [2025-05-31T08:59:46.928Z] "GET /ready HTTP/1.1" 200 - 0 5 0 0 "-" "kube-probe/1.32" "150be6e0-1798-4db9-ae88-6cc5a77cf3fd" "internalkourier" "127.0.0.1:9901"
    ```

pullRequests.body:
<!-- Thanks for sending a pull request! -->

<!-- 
Are you using Knative? If you do, we would love to know!
https://github.com/knative/community/issues/new?template=ADOPTERS.yaml&title=%5BADOPTERS%5D%3A+%24%7BCOMPANY+NAME+HERE%7D

# Changes

<!-- 
Describe your changes here- ideally you can get that description straight from
your descriptive commit message(s)! 

- :gift: Add new feature
- :bug: Fix bug
- :broom: Update or clean up current behavior
- :wastebasket: Remove feature or internal logic
-->

:bug: Refactoring performed on e2e-kind.sh:
- 1: Removed unnecessary namespace specification.
  - This should have been fixed in https://github.com/knative-extensions/net-kourier/pull/1334 🙇 
- 2: Modified to reference rollout status instead of condition.


Regarding point 2:

We are experiencing intermittent failures with GitHub Actions.
- https://github.com/knative-extensions/net-kourier/actions/runs/15393564244/job/43308474380
- https://github.com/knative-extensions/net-kourier/actions/runs/15344106223/job/43176455027

From what I’ve observed, the following behaviour occurs:
- GitHub Actions fail during tests that configure path prefixes.
- Until the path prefix configuration is reflected in the Pod (externalAuthz), a 403 error occurs.

The `condition=Available` check likely doesn't guarantee that the pod has completed its rollout.
Therefore, I have modified the configuration to verify the rollout status instead.


<!--
In addition, categorize the changes you're making using the "/kind" Prow command, example:

/kind <kind>

Supported kinds are: api-change, bug, cleanup, deprecation, removal, documentation, enhancement, performance

-->
/kind bug

# Changes

Update the controller DNS name to use the short form of the DNS that should be resolvable within the namespace as all components involved should be in the same namespace.

/kind bug

This fixes issues if deployed outside of `knative-serving` namespace (via the operator etc.) as the original is hardcoded with that namespace.  As in that case the gateways will never become healthy as they cannot communicate with the incorrect address in this configuration.  The short form change here should be functional in all cases.

pullRequests.title: Move conflict-resync into ObserveDeletion handler
pullRequests.body:
<!-- Thanks for sending a pull request! -->

# Changes

Without the resync being triggered by the ObserveDeletion handler, we
might have a race between triggering the resync and actually reconciling
the deletion of an Ingress. So the resynced keys might see a conflict
yet again because the deletion was not yet reconciled when the resync
happened. This puts things into the correct order.

/hold

To try this out properly in Serving.

pullRequests.title: Globally reconcile conflicted Ingresses on deletion
pullRequests.body:
<!-- Thanks for sending a pull request! -->

# Changes

Tested in https://github.com/knative/serving/pull/11091

If an Ingress goes into a conflicting state it might never come back from that because nothing triggers its reconcilation again. Most notably: The deletion of the Ingress that caused the conflict should trigger reconcilation.

So this adds a global reconcilation of all Ingresses that are in a conflict state when any Ingress is removed.

<!-- Please include the 'why' behind your changes if no issue exists -->

**Release Note**

<!--
:page_facing_up: If this change has user-visible impact, write a release note in the block
below. Include the string "action required" if additional action is required of
users switching to the new release, for example in case of a breaking change.

Write as if you are speaking to users, not other Knative contributors. If this
change has no user-visible impact, no release-note is needed.
-->
```release-note
Fixed a bug where an Ingress that is in a conflict never resumes from that state without kicking it.
```

releases.name: v1.21.0
releases.body:
knative-extensions/net-kourier@release-1.21
## Changes by Kind

### Enhancement

- Migrated distributed tracing from Zipkin to OpenTelemetry Protocol (OTLP).
  
  Users with tracing enabled must update their `config-kourier` ConfigMap to use the new OTLP configuration. The old `tracing-collector-full-endpoint` key has been removed. Use `tracing-endpoint`, `tracing-protocol`, and `tracing-sampling-rate` instead. (#1395, @linkvt)

### Bug or Regression

- Fix handling of endpoints with no ready addresses (#1397, @linkvt)
- Fixed duplicate domain handling in Kourier when multiple Ingress rules specify the same host with different paths (e.g., app traffic + ACME challenges). Routes are now correctly merged into a single VirtualHost per domain, satisfying Envoy's uniqueness requirement. (#1399, @linkvt)
- Fixed path matching priority when an Ingress has multiple rules for the same domain. (#1412, @linkvt)

### Uncategorized

- Add ability to set a list of listening IP addresses. This enables the usage of kourier in clusters with IPv4 and IPv6. (#1375, @agallegoz)
- Adjust readiness and liveness probes timeout to improve Kourier gateway stability (#1410, @dsimansk)
- Migrated from using the deprecated Endpoints API to EndpointSlice API. (#1402, @linkvt)
- Read the service-access-log-template field without trimming (#1373, @ystkfujii)

## Dependencies

### Added
- github.com/golang-jwt/jwt/v5: [v5.3.0](https://github.com/golang-jwt/jwt/tree/v5.3.0)
